howItWorks.intro: This forex lot size calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.
rules: Lot Size = (Account Balance × Risk %) / (Stop Loss Pips × Pip Value)
howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: What lot size should I use?
faq.answer: Most risk management guidelines suggest risking 1-2% of your account per trade. The lot size depends on your stop loss distance and account size.

faq.question: What is a micro lot?
faq.answer: A micro lot is 1,000 units of currency (0.01 standard lots). It is the smallest tradeable lot size at most brokers.

faq.question: Is the Forex Lot Size Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What account balance should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate account balance value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Forex Lot Size Calculator?
sections.body: The Forex Lot Size Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your account balance, risk per trade, stop loss, you get instant results including risk amount, lot size, units. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting risk amount right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your account balance and need to find the right risk amount. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Account Balance ($)
howToSteps.description: Start by entering your account balance — this is the primary input for the calculation.

howToSteps.title: Fill In Additional Details
howToSteps.description: Complete the remaining fields: risk per trade, stop loss, pip value per lot. Each value refines the calculation for greater accuracy.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your risk amount, lot size, units. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your account balance before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both risk amount and lot size — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to get accurate results.
If you're unsure about your pip value per lot, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
